title: Introduction to Factor Markets
description: Practice what you have learned about the supply of labor, the demand for labor, and the marginal revenue product of labor in this exercise.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is a firm’s demand for labor?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the firm’s marginal revenue product of labor ($MRP_L$) curve
the firm’s marginal factor cost ($MFC$) curve
the firm’s marginal revenue ($MR$) curve
the firm’s marginal revenue product of capital ($MRP_K$) curve
the firm’s long-run average total cost ($LRATC$) curve

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes a firm’s marginal revenue product of labor ($MRP_L$)?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the marginal product labor multiplied by the marginal revenue gained
the additional revenue gained from the sale of one more unit
the additional cost associated with using an additional unit of labor
the horizontal sum of every firm’s demand for labor
the additional output gained from using one more unit of input

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes a firm’s marginal revenue product of capital ($MRP_K$)?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the marginal product of capital multiplied by the marginal revenue gained
the additional revenue gained from the sale of one more unit
the additional cost associated with using an additional unit of capital
the horizontal sum of every firm’s demand for capital
the additional output gained from using one more unit of capital

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes how to calculate the marginal revenue product of labor for a firm?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: $\text{Marginal revenue product of labor} (MRP_L) = \text{marginal revenue} (MR) \times \text{marginal product of labor} (MP_L)$
$\text{Marginal revenue product of labor} (MRP_L) =\dfrac{\text{marginal product of labor} ($MP_L$)}{ \text{Price of labor} (PL)}$
$\text{Marginal revenue product of labor} (MRP_L) =\dfrac{\text{Marginal Product of Labor}(MPL)}{ \text{Marginal Product of Capital} (MPK)}$
$\text{Marginal revenue product of labor} (MRP_L) = \dfrac{P_L}{P_K}$
$\text{Marginal revenue product of labor} (MRP_L) =\text{Marginal Factor Cost of Capital} (MFC_K) \times \text{Marginal Factor Cost of Labor }(MFC_L)$

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the marginal revenue product of labor ($MRP_L$)?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the additional revenue a firm earns if it uses one more unit of labor.
the additional cost of using one more unit of labor.
The additional revenue a firm earns from selling one more unit of output
The additional revenue a firm earns divided by the quantity produced
The amount of capital needed to balance out labor.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes the behavior that creates a labor supply curve?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: an individual maximizing utility in choosing between labor and leisure
a firm choosing its output to maximize profit
an individual maximizing utility by choosing what goods to consume
a country choosing a point on its production possibilities curve
a firm comparing the marginal revenue product of labor to the price of labor

assessment_items.question_content:
**If the wage rate decreases, which of the following is the most likely outcome?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: people decrease the quantity of labor supplied; firms increase the quantity of labor demanded.
people increase the quantity of labor supplied; firms decrease the quantity of labor demanded
people don’t change the quantity of labor supplied; firms increase the quantity of labor demanded
people decrease their leisure and increase their supply of labor
people decide to equalize their leisure and labor usage

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes what happens in the market for labor if the wage rate increases?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The quantity of labor supplied increases; the quantity of labor demanded decreases
The quantity of labor supplied doesn’t change; the quantity of labor demanded increases
The quantity of labor supplied decreases; the quantity of labor demanded increases
Labor supply increases; labor demand increases
The quantity of labor supplied increases; the supply of labor increases

assessment_items.question_content:
The table below gives output, marginal product, and average product of a firm producing novelty T-shirts using different numbers of workers. The firm produces t-shirts in a perfectly competitive market where the price of a t-shirt is $\$10$:

Units of labor | Output| Marginal Product | Average Product
-|-|-
0 | 0 | - | -
1 | 90 | 90 | 90
2 | 200 | 110 | 100  
3 | 330 | 130 | 110
4 | 400 | 70 | 50
5 | 450 | 50 | 90
6 | 420 | 30 | 70

**What is the marginal revenue product of labor ($MRP_L$) of the fourth worker?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Marginal revenue product of labor $(MRP_L)$  is marginal revenue $(MR)$ multiplied by the marginal product of labor $(MP_L)$:

$MRP_L = MR \times MP_L$
This firm operates in a perfectly competitive market, so marginal revenue is the same as price no matter how many t-shirts are sold:

$\begin{align}MR &= P\\\\
& =\$10\end{align}$
The marginal product of the fourth unit of labor is 70 units:

Units of labor | Output| Marginal Product | Average Product
-|-|-
$0$ | $0$ | - | -
$1$ | $90$ | $90$ | $90$
$2$ | $200$ | $110$ | $100  $
$3$ | $330$ | $130$ | $110$
$4$ | $400$ | $\color{red}{70}$ | $50$
$5$ | $450$ | $50$ | $90$
$6$ | $420$ | $30$ | $70$


The $MRP_L$ is $\$700$:

$\begin{align}MRP_L &= P \times MP_L\\\\
&= \$10 \times 70\\\\
&=\$700\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Blammo Inc. produces greeting cards in a perfectly competitive market. The table below shows the total number of greeting cards produced using different amounts of labor. The market price for a greeting card is \$4.

Units of labor | Total produced | Marginal Product
-|-|-
0 | 0 | -
1 | 100 | 100
2 | 220 | 120
3 | 300 | 80
4 | 360 | 60
5 | 400 | 40
6 | 400 | 0

**What is the marginal revenue product of labor (MRP_L) of the fifth worker?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$MRP_L$  is marginal revenue ($MR$) multiplied by the marginal product of labor ($MP_L$):

$MRP_L = MR \times MP_L$
Blammo Inc. operates in a perfectly competitive market, so marginal revenue is the same as price no matter how many greeting cards are sold:

$\begin{align}MR &= P\\\\
&=\$4\end{align}$
The marginal product of the fifth unit of labor is $40$ units:

Units of labor | Total produced | Marginal Product
-|-|-
0 | 0 | -
1 | 100 | 100
2 | 220 | 120
3 | 300 | 80
4 | 360 | 60
5 | 400 | **40**
6 | 400 | 0

$MRP_L$ is $\$160$.


$\begin{align}MRP_L &= P \times MP_L\\\\
& = \$4 \times 40\\\\
&=\$160\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The table below shows the marginal revenue product of labor ($MRP_L$) of a firm producing pillows. The firm uses various amounts of labor, and it can sell as many pillows as it wants for $\$10$.

Labor | MRP
-|-
$0$  | -
$1$ | $\$300$
$2$ | $\$250$
$3$ | $\$200$
$4$ | $\$150$
$5$ | $\$100$
$6$ | $\$50$
$7$ | $\$0$


**What is the marginal product of the fourth worker?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Marginal revenue product is marginal revenue times marginal product.
If a firm can sell as much as it wants for $\$10$, marginal revenue is $\$10$.

The fourth worker has a marginal revenue product of $\$150$, so it must have a marginal product of $15$ pillows:

$\begin{align} MRP_L &= MR \times MP\\\\
\$150 & = \$10 \times \$150\\\\
\$150 &= \$150\end{align}$

choices.content: $15$ pillows
$50$ pillows
$1$ pillow
$10$ pillows
$20$ pillows
